content: A variable is a named location in memory that holds a value which can change while a program runs. Every variable has a data type, which determines what kind of value it can hold and what operations are valid on it. Common primitive types across languages include integers (whole numbers), floats/doubles (decimal numbers), strings (text), and booleans (true/false). Statically-typed languages (Java, C, C++) require you to declare a variable's type up front; dynamically-typed languages (Python, JavaScript) infer it at runtime, letting the same variable name even hold different types over its lifetime.

quiz.question: Which of these is a dynamically-typed language?
In Java, you must declare a variable's type before using it.
Name the four data types shown in the Python example.

content: A function is a reusable, named block of code that performs a specific task. Functions take zero or more parameters as input and may return a value. Breaking a program into functions avoids repeating code, makes programs easier to test (test one function at a time), and makes intent clearer — a well-named function documents what it does without needing a comment. Parameters are local to the function; variables declared inside a function generally don't exist outside it (this is called scope).

quiz.question: What does 'scope' refer to in the context of functions?
Write a function `is_even(n)` that returns True if n is even.

content: Exception handling lets a program respond to runtime errors (like dividing by zero, or a file that doesn't exist) without crashing. Most languages use a try/catch (or try/except) structure: code that might fail goes in the try block, and code to handle the failure goes in the catch/except block. This separates 'normal' logic from error-handling logic, and lets you fail gracefully — showing a useful message or falling back to a default, instead of the whole program stopping.

quiz.question: Which block always runs, whether or not an exception occurred?
Catching a broad exception type (like `Exception`) is generally better practice than catching a specific one.
